title: Batching & SGD — Stochastic Noise as Feature
summary: Three-layer model: parallelism is the engineering reason for batching, SGD is the statistical license, gradient noise is the optimization feature (regularization, escape bad minima, generalization).
key_insights: SGD = statistical reason batching is valid (unbiased gradient estimate)
Gradient noise = optimization feature, not side effect — regularizer, prevents sharp minima
Full-batch GD gets stuck in sharp valleys; SGD noise finds broad, stable basins
Very large batches hurt generalization — noise reduction removes the regularizer
Analogy: SGD = noisy compass in fog — less precise but avoids traps

mathematical_core.unbiasedness: $\mathbb{E}[g_B(\theta)] = \nabla L(\theta)$ — mini-batch gradient is unbiased estimate

derivations.title: Gradient noise as implicit regularization

steps.name: Effect on optimization
steps.math: Update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta(\nabla L + \epsilon_t)$. Equivalent to GD on a smoothed loss: noise averages over nearby values, making sharp minima less attractive.

steps.name: Sharp vs flat minima
steps.math: Sharp: high curvature, loss increases rapidly under perturbation. SGD noise perturbs $\theta$ → model escapes. Flat: low curvature, noise doesn't push model out → stable.

steps.name: Generalization connection
steps.math: Flat minima generalize better (robust to distribution shift $\approx$ parameter perturbation). SGD biases toward flat → better generalization.

derivations.consequence: SGD noise is a built-in regularizer favoring solutions that generalize. Removing it (large batch, low LR) often hurts test performance.

derivations.title: Batch size — learning rate scaling law

steps.name: Linear scaling rule
steps.math: Multiply batch size by $k$ → multiply LR by $k$. Gradient variance $\downarrow k$, larger LR compensates.

steps.name: Breakdown at large batch
steps.math: Linear scaling works up to critical batch size $B_{\text{crit}}$. Beyond it: diminishing returns.

steps.name: Critical batch size
steps.math: $B_{\text{crit}} \approx B_{\text{noise}} / \epsilon^2$. Empirically: $B_{\text{crit}}$ grows with model size.

derivations.consequence: Optimal batch size exists for each model/dataset. Larger is not always better.

geometric_properties: Loss landscape has many local minima — SGD noise helps navigate between them
Flat minima have large basin of attraction — SGD preferentially finds these
Noise scale $\eta^2 \sigma^2 / |B|$ determines the 'temperature' of exploration
statistical_properties: Gradient variance $\text{Var}(g_B) = \sigma^2 / |B|$ — monitor to choose batch size
Signal-to-noise ratio: $\|\nabla L\| / \sqrt{\text{Var}(g_B)}$ — should be $> 1$ for meaningful updates
SNR $\gg 1$: batch too large. SNR $\ll 1$: batch too small
numerical_properties: Batch size must fit in GPU memory: $B \times n \times d \times 4$ bytes (plus activations)
Gradient accumulation: simulate large batch by summing gradients over multiple forward passes
Mixed precision: fp16 forward/backward, fp32 accumulation reduces memory per sample
application_relevance: Batch size is a design decision: too large loses regularization, too small wastes compute.
